content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN strings. Each string SiS_iSi​ has length MMM and consists only of lowercase english letters.

Chef likes palindromes, and would like each SiS_iSi​ to be a palindrome. To achieve this, he can perform the following move:


Pick an index iii such that 1≤i<N1 \leq i \lt N1≤i<N and an index jjj such that 1≤j≤M1 \leq j \leq M1≤j≤M.
Then, swap the jthj^{th}jth character of string SiS_iSi​ with the jthj^{th}jth character of string Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1​.
Informally, Chef can swap the jthj^{th}jth character of any two consecutive strings.

Find the **minimum** number of moves required to make every string a palindrome. Print −1-1−1 if it is not possible to achieve this.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of strings and their length, respectively.
The ithi^{th}ith of the next NNN lines contains the string SiS_iSi​.




Output Format

Print a single integer on a new line as the answer to each test case: the **minimum** number of swaps needed to make every SiS_iSi​ a palindrome, or −1-1−1 if this is not possible.
constraints: Each SiS_iSi​ contains only lowercase english letters.
The sum of N×MN\times MN×M across all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055\cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.input:
4
3 2
ac
ba
cb
2 3
xkx
ptp
4 4
abaa
baba
baab
aabb
4 4
abaa
baba
baab
aaab
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can make all the strings palindromes in two operations:

First, swap the second characters of S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​. Now, the strings are {aa, bc, cb}\{\texttt{aa, bc, cb}\}{aa, bc, cb}
Then, swap the first characters of S2S_2S2​ and S3S_3S3​. Now, the strings are {aa, bb, cc}\{\texttt{aa, bb, cc} \}{aa, bb, cc}, which are all palindromes.

Test case 222: Both strings are already palindromes.
Test case 333: It can be shown that it's impossible to make all 444 strings simultaneously palindromes.
Test case 444: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows:

Swap the first characters of S3S_3S3​ and S4S_4S4​. Now, the strings are {abaa, baba, aaab, baab}\{\texttt{abaa, baba, aaab, baab} \}{abaa, baba, aaab, baab}.
Swap the second characters of S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​. Now, the strings are {aaaa, bbba, aaab, baab}\{\texttt{aaaa, bbba, aaab, baab} \}{aaaa, bbba, aaab, baab}.
Swap the fourth characters of S2S_2S2​ and S3S_3S3​. Now, the strings are {aaaa, bbbb, aaaa, baab}\{\texttt{aaaa, bbbb, aaaa, baab} \}{aaaa, bbbb, aaaa, baab}.

Thus, the final strings are all palindromes. It can be proven that we cannot achieve all palindromes in less than 333 moves.
